entities.description: The May 1996 U-M electronic edition represented by the current Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse item. Its header names Skeat's 1867 edition and OTA A*-1047-A, omits printed front matter and editorial notes, and records two caesura decisions.
entities.label: University of Michigan PPlCreed electronic edition
notes: The current U-M item exposes the full text and a two-part table of contents.
Its current rights statement is recorded separately from the restrictive historical availability notice embedded in the pinned XML.

access.accessMethod: Consult the Finna union-catalog record; request or purchase a physical copy through a holding library or bookseller.
access.entityLabel: Greenwood Press 1969 reprint of Skeat's edition
notes: No openly downloadable scan of the 1969 reprint was verified; the older 1867 edition is publicly available in full.

bibliographic.citation: Pierce the Ploughmans Crede (about 1394 A.D.), ed. Walter W. Skeat, EETS Original Series 30 (New York: Greenwood Press, 1969), reprint of the 1867 edition.
bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society, Original Series 30
bibliographic.title: Pierce the Ploughmans Crede (about 1394 A.D.)

entities.description: The New York Greenwood Press reprint identified by the OTA header as the immediate transcription source. It reproduces the 1867 EETS edition; the precise rights status of the 1969 carrier and any new matter remains unverified.
entities.label: Greenwood Press 1969 reprint of Skeat's edition

rights.basis: The underlying 1867 edition is public domain in the United States, but the reviewed sources did not establish the copyright status of the 1969 reprint's carrier, typography, or any new matter.
rights.component: 1969 reprint carrier and any newly added matter

bibliographic.citation: Pierce the Ploughmans Crede (about 1394 A.D.), transcribed and edited from MS. Trin. Coll., Cam., R.3.15, collated with MS. Bibl. Reg. 18 B. XVII and with the old printed copy of 1553, ed. Walter W. Skeat, EETS Original Series 30 (London: N. Trübner & Co., 1867).
bibliographic.pages: Crede text 1-32; notes 33-55; complete volume xx, 75 pages
bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society, Original Series 30
bibliographic.title: Pierce the Ploughmans Crede (about 1394 A.D.)

entities.description: The source edition for the later Greenwood reprint. Skeat says he transcribed Trinity R.3.15, collated Royal 18 B XVII and the 1553 print, used Wright's 1856 edition, and represented expanded manuscript abbreviations in italics. The Crede text occupies printed pages 1-32 and has 855 verse lines.
entities.label: Walter W. Skeat's 1867 EETS edition
notes: Skeat also appends the separate rhyming poem God Spede the Plough; it is outside the cataloging boundary of PPlCreed.

rights.basis: The edition was published in 1867, well before the current United States public-domain cutoff for published works.

bibliographic.citation: The Vision and Creed of Piers Ploughman, ed. Thomas Wright, 2 vols., 2nd rev. ed. (London: John Russell Smith, 1856).
bibliographic.title: The Vision and Creed of Piers Ploughman
bibliographic.volume: 2 vols.; Crede text in vol. II

entities.description: The second and revised two-volume edition that Skeat says was of very great service. Complete public scans of both volumes are cached. Volume I discusses the Crede's date, subject, witnesses, and early prints; volume II contains the Crede text at printed pages 451-501 and its notes at pages 565-587.
entities.label: Thomas Wright's 1856 second revised edition
notes: Wright's notes compare Crede readings with an old printed edition, but the reviewed statements do not securely identify whether his immediate copy-text was Wolfe 1553, Rogers 1561, or a later reprint; no directional source edge is asserted.

Wright's 1856 claim that Royal and Trinity were both written after the first print is retained only as an edition-history claim: the current British Library catalog dates Royal to the first quarter of the sixteenth century, before Wolfe 1553.

rights.basis: The edition was published in 1856, well before the current United States public-domain cutoff for published works.

entities.description: A composite paper codex. The Crede is the final twelve-leaf item, in the hand associated with Part I, and is complete at the IIIF labels fols. 317r-328r. Skeat used this witness as his base text.

holding.notes: The work is labeled fols. 317r-328r in the complete IIIF sequence, canvases 629-651.

entities.label: Trinity College Cambridge MS R.3.15
notes: The catalog's 316-folio extent and the IIIF labels through f328 have not yet been reconciled.

physicalDescription.extent: Catalog description: 316 folios; IIIF labels continue through f328
notes: The catalog records 30 lines to a page and multiple hands.

rights.basis: The anonymous medieval work and sixteenth-century manuscript text are outside United States copyright term; modern photographs are a separate component.

access.entityLabel: Skeat's reconstructed common exemplar

dateStatements.value: before the surviving Trinity, Royal, and 1553 witnesses

entities.description: A hypothetical common source inferred by Skeat for Trinity R.3.15, Royal 18 B XVII, and the 1553 Wolfe print. It is a stemmatic reconstruction rather than an identified or extant codex.
entities.label: Skeat's reconstructed common exemplar
notes: No copied-from relation is asserted for Harley 78 because the reviewed evidence did not place that fragment in Skeat's reconstructed three-witness stemma.

bibliographic.pages: downloadable PDF pages 2-50: introduction 2-8, text 9-35, notes 36-50
bibliographic.title: Six Ecclesiastical Satires

entities.description: A later scholarly edition in Six Ecclesiastical Satires. Dean uses Trinity microfilm as base, corrects it from Royal and the 1553 print, and consults Wright 1856 and Skeat 1867. It is a supporting comparison source, not upstream of the repository XML, and numbers the poem as 850 lines.
entities.label: James M. Dean's 1991 METS edition

rights.basis: METS states that digital editions are for individual use and directs users seeking to reprint an edition to its Executive Director; it separately licenses XML under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

next_steps: Ask the University of Michigan and Oxford Text Archive which statement governs redistribution of the exact legacy PPlCreed transcription and markup.
Ask the CME repository maintainer whether the Unlicense is intended to cover source text data and XML markup as well as software.
Until clarified, keep each dated distribution layer separate and do not infer that the most permissive statement overrides the others.
openQuestions.question: How should the pinned XML's personal-scholarly-use notice be reconciled with the current U-M No Copyright - United States label, the current OTA CC BY-NC-SA 3.0 license, and the CME repository's software-worded Unlicense?

next_steps: Consult later textual studies that collate all complete witnesses and the Harley fragment.
Preserve Skeat attribution and tentative status until an independent stemma is found.
openQuestions.question: Can Skeat's reconstructed common exemplar and the relationships among Trinity, Royal, and Wolfe be confirmed or revised from later stemmatic scholarship?

next_steps: Collate Wright's 1856 text against Wolfe 1553, Rogers 1561, and Whitaker's 1814 reprint.
Consult Wright's first-edition production records or later bibliographical scholarship for an explicit copy-text statement.
Do not add a directional source relation from Wright until the old printed edition named in his notes is identified.
openQuestions.question: Which early print or reprint supplied Wright's immediate copy-text for the Crede in his 1856 second revised edition?

next_steps: Collate Dean's numbering against Skeat's printed lines 1-855 and record each joined or split long line.
Keep source-specific line locators explicit rather than treating either numbering system as universal.
openQuestions.question: Which editorial joining or splitting decisions account for Dean's 850-line text versus Skeat and DIMEV's 855-line count?

next_steps: Ask Trinity whether the catalog extent excludes blanks, inserted leaves, or later foliation strata.
Retain both the catalog extent and exact IIIF labels without normalizing one into the other.
openQuestions.question: Why does the Trinity catalog state 316 folios while the complete IIIF sequence labels leaves through f328?

next_steps: Recheck the British Library catalog and digitised-manuscripts services after platform migrations.
Seek a quotation for whole-manuscript imaging where feasible, while requesting publication terms separately.
Check for newly open copies of STC 19904 beyond the subscription EEBO surrogate.
openQuestions.question: Will complete public digital facsimiles become available for Royal 18 B XVII, Harley 78, or the 1553 Wolfe print?

next_steps: Inspect a physical 1969 copy's copyright page and compare it with the 1867 volume.
Do not infer the reprint carrier's rights from the public-domain status of Skeat's underlying edition.
openQuestions.question: What copyright or contractual restrictions, if any, attach specifically to the 1969 Greenwood reprint carrier and its new matter?

reviewNotes: Skeat's printed poem has 855 verse lines. The XML contains 856 L elements because XML L 847 encodes the printed running head GOD AMEND ALL FALSE FRIARS ! as though it were verse; XML L 848-856 therefore correspond to printed lines 847-855.
The printed final AMEN. is not absent from the XML: it follows the 856 L elements as a CLOSER. It is excluded only from the raw L-element count and verse-line stream.

The supplied XML heading Lines 101 through 200 encloses L 101-201, while the next division begins at L 202. Source locators therefore use actual element and printed-line boundaries rather than trusting every supplied heading.

The second top-level division, headed CREDO., is the poem's intrinsic conclusion: after the narrator asks Piers to teach him the Creed in lines 791-794, Piers answers in printed lines 795-855 and the closer.

DIMEV's Royal witness entry gives complete folios, incipit, and explicit but repeats the Harley fragment note Lines 172-207 only. The official British Library catalog and the edition evidence establish Royal as complete; the repeated note is retained as a DIMEV data defect.

Access, copyright status, licenses, and provider conditions are modeled separately. A public page or public-domain historical text does not by itself establish unrestricted redistribution of a modern image file.

The Trinity catalog describes 316 folios, while the IIIF labels run through f328. This unresolved descriptive mismatch does not affect the directly inspected work boundary at labeled fols. 317r-328r, canvases 629-651.

lineage.summary: The repository XML is a preserved version of the University of Michigan May 1996 electronic edition, whose header associates it with Oxford Text Archive accession A*-1047-A. The current OTA 1047 header says its text was transcribed from Greenwood Press's 1969 reprint of Walter W. Skeat's 1867 EETS edition. Skeat based his text on Trinity College Cambridge R.3.15 and collated British Library Royal 18 B XVII and Reynold Wolfe's 1553 print; he also acknowledged substantial use of Thomas Wright's 1856 edition. Trinity and Royal are complete manuscript witnesses, Harley 78 preserves only lines 172-207, and Wolfe is an early printed textual witness. Skeat hypothesized that Trinity, Royal, and Wolfe descend from one common exemplar, but that stemma is an editorial reconstruction, not a confirmed physical chain. Whole public surrogates have been cached where available, including the complete 656-canvas Trinity IIIF object; no complete public facsimile was verified for Royal, Harley, or the 1553 print.

catalogingSubject.label: Piers the Plowman's Crede
catalogingSubject.scope_note: One anonymous late-fourteenth-century alliterative poem. The XML's second DIV1, headed CREDO., is the intrinsic concluding section in which Piers answers the narrator's request; it is not a separately cataloged work.

contentParts.label: The narrator's search and Piers's antifraternal speech
notes: The eight nested DIV2 elements are supplied hundred-line navigation groups; one has an incorrect displayed range and none is treated as an authored subdivision.
contentParts.summary: The narrator seeks instruction from the four orders of friars, encounters Piers, and hears his extended critique of mendicant wealth and hypocrisy before asking to be taught the Creed.

contentParts.label: CREDO. and conclusion

locators.value: DIV1.2; L 795-846 and 848-856; CLOSER AMEN. (L 847 is a non-verse running head)

notes: The XML heading marks an intrinsic rhetorical transition, not a second independent work.
contentParts.summary: Piers teaches the narrator the Creed and the poem closes with doctrinal reflection, an authorial correction formula, and a prayer that faithful friars be saved and false friars amended.

assertion.notes: Dean uses Walter Brut's 1393 trial as the terminus a quo and places composition before 1401.

notes: The sixteenth-century dates of the complete surviving manuscripts are witness dates, not composition dates.

extent.description: One verse work in two XML DIV1 sections: 794 verse lines before the CREDO. heading and 61 verse lines in the intrinsic Creed-and-conclusion section. Raw encoding metrics are 856 L elements plus one CLOSER: 855 L elements represent verse, one L element is a printed running head, and the CLOSER preserves AMEN. The eight supplied DIV2 line-range groups are navigation, not intellectual verse groups; the group labeled Lines 101 through 200 actually includes XML L 101-201.

formStatements.description: Unrhymed alliterative long-line verse. The terminal AMEN. is encoded as a closer rather than a numbered verse line.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: Brief Latin liturgical words and formulas are embedded vocabulary, not a second work-level language.
The work is anonymous. Skeat's title-page association with Langland's Piers Plowman and older catalog attributions must not be converted into authorship.
The first-quarter-sixteenth-century Royal manuscript and the possibly later Trinity hand are transmission evidence, not substitutes for the 1393-1401 composition date.
The running head encoded as XML L 847 is excluded from every intellectual verse-line metric.
The current OTA, U-M, MEDictionary, repository, scan, and manuscript-image rights statements apply to different layers and are recorded only in the linked lineage manifest.

next_steps: Consult a dialectological study or Linguistic Atlas profile specific to the Crede.
Distinguish authorial localization from the copying dialects of Trinity R.3.15, Royal 18 B XVII, Harley 78, and the 1553 print.
notes: Southwest Midlands is retained as a tentative cataloging region because Dean phrases it as probable authorial origin rather than a full linguistic demonstration.
openQuestions.question: How narrowly can the poem's authorial dialect be localized independently of the sixteenth-century witness spellings?

next_steps: Collate Dean's line numbering against Skeat's printed lines 1-855 and record every joined or split long line.
Keep source-specific line locators explicit rather than treating either numbering system as universal.
openQuestions.question: Which editorial joining or splitting decisions account for Dean's 850-line text versus Skeat and DIMEV's 855-line count?

assertion.notes: The source supports probable authorial origin, not a complete dialectological localization independent of the late witnesses.

notes: Dean says the probable Southwest Midland author had London connections; this is not a claim that the poem was composed in London.

assertion.notes: No securely identified medieval author is known.

scope.description: Editorial responsibility for the 1867 EETS source edition, not medieval authorship.

subjects.label: The Apostles' Creed and lay religious instruction

summaries.value: An anonymous Middle English alliterative satire in which a narrator seeking to learn the Apostles' Creed consults representatives of the four mendicant orders. Their rivalry, wealth, and demands for money expose their failure as spiritual teachers. The poor plowman Piers instead explains true Christian belief and recites the Creed, joining antifraternal polemic to a Wycliffite ideal of apostolic poverty and plain faith.

The first XML DIV1 contains lines 1-794; after the narrator asks Piers to teach him, the second DIV1 headed CREDO. continues the same poem. It contains printed verse lines 795-855 and the terminal closer AMEN. The division is retained as a useful locator but does not create a second work.

The work has 855 verse lines in Skeat's printed lineation and DIMEV. The XML has 856 L elements because the page-32 running head GOD AMEND ALL FALSE FRIARS ! was mistakenly encoded as L N=847. XML L labels 848-856 therefore correspond to printed lines 847-855. The final AMEN. is preserved separately as CLOSER and is not a verse line. Dean's later METS edition numbers 850 lines, an edition-specific lineation retained as a discrepancy rather than substituted for the source edition's extent.

notes: Modern discovery title. The corpus source and Skeat use the historical spelling 'Pierce the Ploughmans Crede'.

scope.description: Heading of the first XML DIV1.

notes: Discovery form normalized only enough to replace thorn with 'th'; witness and edition spellings vary.
titles.value: Cros and Curteis Crist this bygynnynge spede

work.author: Anonymous

Their rivalry, wealth, and demands for money expose their failure as spiritual teachers. The poor plowman Piers instead explains true Christian belief and recites the Creed, joining antifraternal polemic to a Wycliffite ideal of apostolic poverty and plain faith.
